segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an energetic, driving electronic background music as a sleek black Brabus 900 sedan, its polished surface reflecting bright lights, is displayed at an auto show with a large 'BRABUS' sign and '900' projected on the floor. A deep male voice introduces 'Deluxe tuning made in Germany,' explaining that 'At the heart of the Ruhr Valley, Brabus transforms Mercedes-Benz brand vehicles into driving powerhouses.' The scene transitions to an aerial view of the modern Brabus facility, showcasing rows of luxury vehicles parked outside, as the voice details the company's maxim: 'more displacement, more horsepower, more torque.' Inside the workshop, a white Brabus G-Wagen is shown with its hood open, revealing a powerful engine, while a technician works nearby. The voice continues, stating they'll 'check and see if the car reaches its speed of 300 kilometers an hour,' emphasizing '900 horsepower. Performance tuning at any cost.' A black Brabus G-Wagen is then seen being worked on, with a mechanic adjusting components under the hood, as the narrator mentions the vehicles can be worth 'half a million or even possibly one million euros.' The black Brabus sedan is then shown on a dynamometer, undergoing performance testing, accompanied by intense engine revving, tire squeal, and powerful acceleration sounds, with a technician monitoring the process. The deep voice highlights that 'The high-end tuners of the Ruhr Valley have been performing supreme precision work for more than 40 years.' A man in a Brabus-branded vest speaks to the camera, gesturing, and is heard briefly speaking in German, which the narrator then translates/reiterates, 'You can't be off by even one or two millimeters.' The video cuts to a close-up of a mechanic installing a large yellow brake caliper onto a wheel, followed by another mechanic using a power tool on a car's interior, as the narrator explains, 'New components are developed and manufactured in the workshop using state-of-the-art technology.' A technician uses a 3D scanner on a car part, then a hand sands a white car part in a spray booth. The final scenes show a mechanic working on an engine with a tattooed arm, and a wide shot of the high-tech workshop with CNC machines and various tools, emphasizing precision engineering. The video concludes with a close-up of a hand using a tool to adjust a component on a car's interior, as a distinct male voice exclaims 'Toys for the boys,' which the narrator then repeats, highlighting the meticulous craftsmanship involved in Brabus modifications.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a CNC machine precisely machining a complex engine block, highlighting the high-precision manufacturing process, as a narrator explains that in the engine department, fine tuning is carried out on a nano scale. The scene transitions to a technician in a black shirt, focused on assembling or inspecting a large engine component, embodying the different level of know-how required for such precision. An aerial view then reveals a modern facility with the text 'THE HIGH-END TUNERS FROM THE RUHR VALLEY' overlaid, establishing the location and prestige of the operation, which the narrator identifies as the tuning premises in the city of Bottrop. The camera pans over a vast parking lot filled with luxury vehicles, including numerous black and white Brabus-modified Mercedes-Benz models, showcasing the scale of production. A man in a black Brabus-branded puffer jacket, representing the tuning experts, is seen inspecting a white Brabus G-Wagen, removing protective coverings from its windows and rear, and then entering the vehicle, as the narrator details that new base vehicles undergo a quality inspection for paintwork damage, dents, and other transit damage upon arrival. He drives the G-Wagen through the lot, passing other high-end models, before entering a 'SoftCare' car wash tunnel. The vehicle is then shown being washed automatically, with water and soap spraying over its body, indicating a final cleaning step before delivery or display, with the narrator explaining that dirt is taboo in the garages to prevent impurities from compromising high-performance tuning parts, though he refers to the vehicle as a Maybach. The sequence ends with the car emerging from the wash, clean and gleaming, ready for the next phase, all set to an upbeat, industrial-sounding background music.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a first-person view from inside a black luxury sedan as it drives into a modern Brabus facility, marked by a prominent 'BRABUS' sign and a Mercedes-Benz emblem. The car enters a spacious, well-lit workshop, where a narrator explains that around 6,500 vehicles are converted annually across 150,000 square meters. The camera pans across the clean, organized environment, revealing numerous high-end vehicles, including multiple Brabus G-Wagens and other customized models, some elevated on lifts. Sven Gramm, Director of Communications, appears in a black Brabus-branded puffer vest, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures. He details the customization options, stating that a performance upgrade costs €1,590 plus VAT, adding 20-30 horsepower in a couple of hours, while a complete overhaul can take three to six months, resulting in a vehicle worth up to one million euros with up to 900 horsepower. The scene then transitions to an elevated 'LEITSTAND' (Control Stand) control room, where a group of men, including Sven Gramm, gather around a large whiteboard covered in task cards and red dots. A narrator explains this is the car tuner's control center, where team leaders and workshop managers meet daily to discuss production status, with around 150 wish lists from car owners worldwide pinned to the wall, emphasizing the global demand for Brabus's personalized vehicles. Gramm points to specific cards, illustrating the workflow, as the team observes attentively, highlighting the professional and coordinated atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a large whiteboard, a 'Whiteboard Task System' covered in 'Arbeitskarte' task cards with red circular markers, organized into columns like 'Kunden Aufträge' and 'Auslieferungstermin 2–6 Wochen'. Steffen Schnur, the Workshop Manager, in a white shirt, gestures and moves these cards, explaining the workflow in German, translated as "We're creating a starry sky for our customers in a G63 Smart Ultimate," detailing a "full leather interior with a complete exterior remodeling." He elaborates on performance upgrades like a "performance boost in kit and a valve controlled exhaust," mentioning some customers desire "700 horsepower, speedometer, roof instruments, door sill panels, and a carbon bonnet" for a "full conversion," and even specific requests like "no chrome or silver details... or any emblems or logos." The camera follows his hand movements, emphasizing the structured process, before transitioning to a row of Brabus G-Wagens, including black and beige models, parked in a clean, well-lit workshop, showcasing their rugged design and distinctive front grilles. A narrator's voice identifies "The customer's favorite model is the Brabus 800 Widestar," noting that "for nearly four decades, the off-road vehicle has been continually fine-tuned in Bottrop," and that this "2.5-ton off-roader can now be upgraded to 900 horsepower." The scene then cuts to dynamic shots of a black Brabus G-Wagen driving on a winding mountain road, highlighting its performance and aggressive styling. This is followed by a CGI animation of a silver Mercedes-AMG G63 rotating on a virtual platform, with the narrator confirming "The Mercedes-AMG G63 forms the base," and on-screen text displaying its specifications: 'V_max 220 km/h' and 'Power: 585 HP', emphasizing its high-performance capabilities, with the narrator reiterating "Top speed of 220 km/h." The sequence blends real-world workshop operations with high-octane driving and digital visualization, all set to background music, to convey Brabus’s precision engineering and luxury performance.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a CGI animation of a silver Mercedes-AMG G63 rotating on a virtual platform, showcasing its design and performance specs of 'Power: 585 HP' and 'Torque 850 Nm', which are simultaneously narrated. The scene transitions to a black Brabus G-Wagen, also in CGI, displaying and narrating its enhanced stats: 'Power: 800 HP', 'Torque 1000 Nm', and 'from 0 to 100 km/h: 4.1 sec', emphasizing its superior performance, accompanied by a car engine revving and whoosh sound. The animation fades to an aerial view of a modern Brabus facility, referred to as 'factory one' where the transformation begins, with a large parking lot and a prominent 'BRABUS' sign. Inside the workshop, a silver Mercedes-AMG G63 is positioned on a hydraulic lift, marked with a '240' tag on the windshield. A technician, identified as a mechanic, in a black shirt operates the lift controls, raising the vehicle. The camera then shows the undercarriage as the technician, wearing blue gloves, removes the original 'standard exhaust system.' He unpacks a new, 'chrome-plated sport exhaust' system from a cardboard box, revealing polished metal components. The technician installs the new exhaust, carefully aligning and securing the dual black-tipped pipes under the vehicle, with the narrator explaining that this modification is for an attention-grabbing look and sound, not to make the SUV faster. Throughout the segment, a driving, slightly dramatic instrumental background music plays.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of two mechanics, including Samir Boulahya, a car mechanic identified by on-screen text, diligently installing a new high-performance exhaust system under a silver Mercedes-AMG G63, elevated on a white 'Hebebühne 4' hydraulic lift. Samir's hands carefully position the polished metal components as he explains in German that "the design and the sound are nicer," highlighting a "sport exhaust" button that puts the car into "sport mode," which "sounds really great." The scene widens to show the G63 on the lift, with Samir operating the gray control panel to adjust its height before entering the driver's seat. A blue plastic bag is taped over one of the exhaust tips. Inside the clean, well-lit Brabus workshop, Samir smiles and speaks, anticipating the test. After an on-screen text indicates "30 minutes later," the "valve-controlled exhaust" has been fitted, increasing the car's value by €7,000, and it's "time for a sound check." The car's engine is heard in "normal mode," then Samir demonstrates the "new component" at the "mere touch of a button," unleashing a significantly louder and more aggressive engine roar. The mechanic, Samir, is visibly satisfied, concluding with a cheerful "Toys for the boys," all set against a backdrop of upbeat background music.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a new exhaust system being installed under a silver Mercedes-AMG G63, one black-tipped pipe covered by a blue plastic bag, as a narrator explains that throughout Europe, motor vehicles, including cars in this performance class, must not exceed 75 decibels—as loud as a washing machine in a spin cycle—and tuners must comply. Inside the G63, Samir Boulahya, a mechanic in a Brabus-branded shirt, adjusts the steering wheel and inspects the dashboard with its blue ambient lighting and partially disassembled center console. The G63 is then seen elevated on a white hydraulic lift, 'Hebebühne 4', with its hood open. Sven Gramm, Director of Communications, appears in a Brabus vest, speaking directly to the camera, emphasizing that Brabus adheres to regulations, stating they would reject requests for a 200-decibel car or a 3-kilogram gold airbag logo due to safety implications. An aerial view of the Brabus Facility showcases a spacious, well-lit workshop filled with multiple Brabus G-Wagens, mostly black and one beige, in various stages of customization. The narrator adds that in the glass hall, fitters are replacing original external paneling and aerodynamic parts to make way for a 'wide star kit' that increases the SUV's value by a five-figure amount. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a glossy black car wheel, reflecting the workshop environment, before transitioning to a wide shot of a spacious, well-lit warehouse-like space filled with stacked tires and boxes, marked with safety signage and industrial equipment. Markus retrieves a large cardboard box, then a silver multi-spoke alloy wheel, which the audio describes as "huge alloy wheels, machined from a single piece." He carries the wheel to a blue and black 'easymount pro' tire mounting machine, carefully placing it on the machine’s hub. He then retrieves a new black 'YOKOHAMA' tire, rolling it to the machine. The audio explains that these "sports tires will later have to keep the two and a half ton car on the road at speeds of up to 240 kilometers per hour," with "the casing, the central framework of the tire, and a special rubber compound" ensuring "the necessary traction." Markus mounts the tire onto the wheel using the machine’s mechanical arms, with close-ups showing the tire being pressed onto the rim and the machine’s clamps securing it. The audio emphasizes the precision required, stating, "Highly tuned vehicles always operate at their physical limits. The tire mechanic cannot afford to make a mistake when mounting them. The consequences would be disastrous." Markus then inflates the tire, checks its pressure, and ensures it is properly seated. Throughout, the clean, organized, and technical workshop environment is visible. Markus, with a focused and professional demeanor, speaks directly to the camera, explaining the process and adding, "Das kann... It could get dangerous then. I wouldn't like to be sitting in the car if a tire bursts."

Slaving away non-stop. They would use a stopwatch to see if we could get the job done in the set time. What matters here, everything has to be top-notch," reinforcing the meticulous standards of the Brabus facility, where other Brabus vehicles are visible in the background.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a clean, well-lit Brabus Facility, where Kai Brekoff from the Development Department, wearing a white Brabus-branded shirt with sponsor logos, meticulously operates a handheld FARO 3D scanner. As he maneuvers the scanner, emitting a blue laser line over the front grille and hood of a glossy black Brabus G-Wagen, a calm male narrator explains that existing spoiler, bumper, or wing kits often don't fit new models, necessitating the design of new attachments. The scanner, connected to a laptop displaying real-time 3D point cloud data with green and blue sections, is crucial for this, as the narrator details how it generates a digital model of relevant vehicle parts to serve as a basis for new designs. Kai occasionally pauses to speak to the camera, gesturing towards the equipment, and with a clear German accent, he elaborates on the technical process: "This large number of dots in a row creates an optical line, and with the coupled motion, we visually get a flat surface." He continues, explaining that a light beam is emitted and recaptured by a receiver camera, allowing them to obtain the correct distances of the scanned data. Throughout, an upbeat electronic background music plays, underscoring the high-tech environment. The narrator concludes by emphasizing that the 3D scanner precisely measures the vehicle contours, highlighting the advanced technology used in Brabus's automotive customization and quality control.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a technician, identified by the narrator as a materials engineer, in a white Brabus-branded shirt and white latex gloves, meticulously pouring a dark, viscous liquid, described by the narrator as a transparent hardener, from a gray bottle into a white bucket positioned on a digital scale for precise measurement. The narrator explains that this hardener, along with a second component, is responsible for the color of the eventual roof instrument holder, emphasizing the need for precise composition of both elements. The white bucket is then transferred into a large, stainless steel 'BRABUS' industrial oven, which the narrator refers to as a vacuum casting machine and curing oven, displaying a temperature of 72.4°C and a timer set for 280 minutes. After the technician operates the control panel, a close-up inside the oven shows the bucket mechanically tilting to pour the liquid into a white rectangular mold, where the two components bind to form polyurethane. Later, the materials engineer removes a cured, yellowish component with a white top, now identified by the narrator as a silicon block and part of the roof instrument holder, from a black tray. He inspects it before fitting it with three filler hoses and placing it into another casting machine. The narrator notes that the black resin, likely referring to the dark liquid, provides excellent dimensional accuracy for high-quality molded parts, and the overall process aims to avoid abnormal cavities caused by air pockets. Throughout the precise manufacturing process in the well-lit workshop, a continuous background music track plays.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a large industrial 'BRABUS' oven, where a technician in a Brabus-branded shirt monitors a digital display showing temperature and a timer. After resting for a further 45 minutes in the curing oven, the technician carefully removes a black tray containing a yellowish cured component, which the narrator identifies as a silicon block, placing it on a wooden board. Mechanical whirring and a new electronic background music track accompany the scene. The technician, now wearing white latex gloves, works on a large brown rubber mold on a rotating platform, adjusting and inspecting it, as the narrator details that the employee may repeat this process between 20 to 80 times before the mold is no longer usable. He then removes a black, glossy, conical plastic part, described as a freshly molded vehicle part, from the mold. The technician uses a heat gun, whose distinct sound is heard, to smooth the edges of the part, applying finishing touches. Two identical black conical parts are then shown side by side on a white table, ready to be installed in a car. The scene transitions to an aerial view of the 'BRABUS Werk 3' facility, which the narrator identifies as the 150,000 square meter tuning premises, showcasing the expansive industrial complex. Inside, the workshop reveals multiple CNC machines, workbenches, and technicians in white shirts, emphasizing the high-tech and organized manufacturing process.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a large, complex engine block being precisely machined inside a CNC machine, with coolant spraying as the cutting tool operates, all within the "engine department" of "Factory 3" where "high-performance engines with up to 900 horsepower are created by hand." A technician in a black Brabus-branded shirt with 'TEAM' on the sleeve carefully monitors this process. The scene shifts to a wider view of the clean, well-lit, and organized workshop, revealing multiple CNC machines and workbenches filled with precision-engineered metal parts, including cylinder heads and custom components, highlighting that in this "mechanical production facility," "the necessary tuning parts are manufactured and finished by the employees." Kai Baumann, Manager CNC Processing, identified by on-screen text and wearing a black Brabus polo shirt with orange 'BRABUS' logos, speaks to the camera, explaining that "prototype parts, standard parts, and development parts are processed and produced here," noting that "the largest part would be a crankcase and the smallest part a shim." He is later seen assisting another technician in loading a large aluminum engine block, specifically a "bare crankcase," into a milling machine, securing it with clamps and adjusting its position. The audio clarifies that "in order to equip engines with more displacement, the mechanics fix the bare crankcases into a milling machine," and that "this 12-cylinder engine will be used to power a limousine." Detailed shots capture the engine block’s internal structure, highlighting the precision of the machining process as "during the milling operation, the diameter of its cylinders are enlarged by reboring," which means "more fuel can ultimately be combusted and this means more power." Kai Baumann's voice further elaborates, stating, "The bore of the engine is being enlarged and the seating is being disassembled because we are also fitting crankshafts." The two technicians work collaboratively, with one operating the machine’s control panel while the other observes, surrounded by tools and parts neatly arranged on blue-handled carts and workbenches, showcasing the technical expertise and teamwork involved in CNC processing for custom Brabus engine components.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a well-lit, organized workshop, where Kai Baumann, Manager CNC Processing, wearing a black Brabus polo shirt, speaks directly to the camera. Behind him, another technician operates a CNC machine. A narrator explains the process, describing it as a purely mechanical operation involving turning and milling. The scene transitions to a close-up of a large aluminum engine block being precisely machined inside a CNC machine, with coolant spraying as the tool cuts the cylinder bores. The narrator details how engine builders use a 3D measuring sensor to determine the exact position of the workpiece, measuring each cylinder precisely to a thousandth of a millimeter, emphasizing that only utmost precision guarantees a perfect final result. A technician, identifiable by his 'BRABUS TEAM' shirt, operates the machine's control panel, adjusting settings, and then uses a handheld probe to measure the engine block's dimensions. The narrator mentions that a colleague loads the machine with milling cutters, which can cost up to 3,000 Euros. The machine continues its operation for one and a half hours, working through the 12 cylinders of the aluminum crankcase. This process enhances the engine block's displacement from 6 to 7.3 liters, an increase of slightly more than 20%. After machining, the component undergoes a honing procedure. The technician, now wearing yellow gloves, carefully removes the engine block from the machine and places it onto a blue-handled cart for transport, all within the clean and organized workshop environment. Throughout, the sounds of industrial machinery and the narrator's voice are present.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a large aluminum engine block, featuring multiple cylinder bores, being handled by motor mechanic Thomas Schreiber, who wears blue nitrile gloves. He carefully positions the block on a Sunnen CK-21 Brabus cylinder honing machine, then secures it with hex bolts using a power tool. The audio explains that this process involves fitting a 'torque plate' which distorts the block as if it were fully assembled, simulating the tension that occurs when cylinder heads are mounted to prevent warping. Thomas then attaches a specialized honing tool, which he describes as 'another very special piece of equipment here' with 'diamond bars,' to the machine. As the machine, labeled with the Brabus logo and model name, is activated, the honing tool is lowered into a cylinder bore. Coolant sprays as the tool rotates and simultaneously moves in and out, smoothing the bore's interior. Thomas monitors the process closely, adjusting settings on the digital control panel, while the audio details that the machine removes 'hundredths of a millimeter' to create a perfect, cylindrical surface, ensuring everything works perfectly afterwards. The clean, well-lit workshop environment, with industrial equipment in the background, underscores the high-precision manufacturing setting and the meticulous technical expertise involved in engine building.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Thomas Schreiber, a motor mechanic in a black Brabus-branded polo shirt and blue nitrile gloves, operating the Sunnen CK-21 Brabus Cylinder Honing Machine. The machine's distinct whirring and spraying sounds accompany him as he carefully secures a large aluminum engine block with hex bolts, then lowers the honing tool into a cylinder bore while coolant sprays to reduce friction, with a flexible black hose extracting debris. A narrator emphasizes the precision required, stating that "one mistake and the 15,000 euro engine block is ruined," and explains that after honing, the inner surface achieves the necessary cross grinding for oil film adhesion and low-friction piston work. A close-up reveals the engine block’s clean, honed cylinder bores. The scene transitions to Sven Bruckschlegel, Head of Engine Design, wearing a black Brabus polo shirt, who inspects the engine block using a borescope connected to a Sony monitor. Sven's voice is heard speaking German, translated by the narrator, explaining, "Here we have a cylinder made from aluminum and silicon crystals, and now we're checking whether the silicon crystals are sufficiently exposed." He adjusts the borescope, pointing to specific areas on the monitor, which displays a magnified, blue-tinted view of the cylinder’s textured interior, as he continues to explain the aluminum. The clean, well-lit industrial workshop environment, with its white walls and tool cabinets, and the ambient sounds of machinery, underscores the technical expertise involved.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a gray Sony monitor, branded 'BRABUS', displaying a blue-tinted, magnified view of a cylinder bore's textured interior, likely from a borescope inspection. A hand enters the frame, pointing at specific features on the screen, indicating a detailed analysis of the surface finish. The scene transitions to a wider shot revealing Sven Bruckschlegel, Head of Engine Design, in a black Brabus polo shirt, adjusting a borescope connected to the monitor while inspecting a large aluminum engine block mounted on a precision stand. A male voice explains that the inspection focuses on the aluminum and silicon crystals within the bore, detailing how the harder silicon crystals are exposed to prevent piston rings from jamming and to allow an oil film to form for optimal lubrication. He further explains that if the silicon relief were too shallow, the aluminum would need to be etched back to ensure pistons don't jam later. The clean, well-lit workshop environment with white walls and a wooden workbench is visible. The camera then focuses on the engine block, showing its multiple cylinder bores and intricate internal structure as it is being inspected. The scene shifts to a table where a row of shiny, precision-machined connecting rods and pistons are laid out. A technician, wearing a black Brabus shirt with orange logos, and a colleague, also in a black Brabus shirt, are seen weighing and marking the components with a digital scale and a red marker. The male voice continues, explaining that these high-end connecting rods are manufactured on-site and, like the motor pistons, are weighed to a tenth of a gram. The technician carefully places a piston on the scale, records the weight, and marks it with a number, as the voice emphasizes that employees sort components by weight to ensure all 12 piston rod pairs have the same mass, which is crucial for smooth engine running. The environment remains consistent with the bright, organized workshop setting, highlighting the precision and attention to detail in high-performance engine assembly, all set to a backdrop of subtle background music.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a technician's hands meticulously placing a piston onto a digital scale for weighing, then marking it with a red marker. The scene expands to a clean, well-lit industrial workshop, where a male technician in a black Brabus-branded polo shirt and a female technician in a similar uniform are engaged in precision work. The male technician gestures towards a large, polished crankshaft mounted on a stand, while the female technician marks a connecting rod with a red marker. A German voice, likely the male technician, explains that this meticulous process ensures the 'perfect running of the high-performance engine,' distinguishing it from large-scale production with greater tolerances. He emphasizes that standard manufacturers aren't interested in the effort of weighing and matching every part, but here they 'figure out the smallest possible tolerance and then further re-work these parts,' calling it 'the best way for this kind of engine.' The camera focuses on the crankshaft as the male technician carefully inserts a small bearing, using a tool for precise placement, then adjusts and inspects it, pointing to specific areas with a pen. An English voiceover then details the components' function: 'The connecting rods connect the pistons to the crankshaft. This too is a special component from the tuning workshop. The crankshaft converts the in and out motion of the pistons into torque. This rotary motion powers the car.' The male technician continues his inspection, and the voiceover highlights their custom design: 'Here we have our own crankshaft, which we developed ourselves. This now has greater stroke as opposed to stock. The connecting rod bearing stud, the middle is further apart from the main bearing stud compared to...' The background reveals an engine block and another technician working on a 3D model, all set to a subtle industrial music, reinforcing the atmosphere of technical expertise and meticulous quality control.

Upbeat, driving electronic music plays in the background as a technician, wearing a black Brabus-branded polo shirt and glasses, inspects and rotates the block, using a tool to adjust or measure components. The camera pans to his hands carefully wiping the cylinder bores with a cloth, emphasizing cleanliness and precision, while the narrator explains how the piston takes a longer route within the cylinder, ascending and descending further. A dial indicator's probe then meticulously moves across the cylinder surface, highlighting the quality control process for this engine, which is designed for greater performance and increased torque rather than just horsepower. The scene transitions to a clean workbench where the technician handles a complex, 3D-printed metallic component with a textured, lattice-like surface, which the narrator identifies as part of the company's own bi-turbo charger system, responsible for an additional 300 horsepower. He then assembles a shiny, multi-bladed turbine-like part to its center, surrounded by neatly arranged connecting rods and pistons. The turbine, described as being powered by exhaust emissions where the exhaust gas turbine drives an axle turbine on the same shaft to compress air and force it into the cylinders, is rotated and inspected closely, showcasing its intricate design and precision engineering within the clean, well-lit workshop environment. The technician’s deliberate and skilled movements underscore his expertise in high-performance engine assembly and component inspection.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens inside a modern, well-lit testing facility, showcasing a sleek black Brabus 900 Sedan secured with orange and black chains and cables on a dynamometer. An industrial fan, connected via a flexible duct, is positioned to simulate airflow. Inside, App Developer Dirk Berkenkopp, wearing a black puffer jacket with red Brabus logos, is focused in the driver's seat. As the test begins, a voice explains in German, "We're looking to see if it actually attains the performance we specify here in the chassis dynamometer," specifically mentioning "800 horsepower." The audio continues, detailing how the chains and cables ensure the "800 horsepower vehicle" does "not go through the wall" by securely anchoring it to the floor. The large industrial fan and two blowers provide "a powerful wind and the supply of fresh air so that the engine and cooling system do not overheat during the sprint," preventing "inaccurate measurement reading and possible engine damage." Close-ups show the car's wheels spinning and the digital dashboard displaying rising speed and RPM, with a yellow and black color scheme. Outside, a monitor labeled 'Konstante Zugkraft' (Constant Traction) shows real-time performance data like torque, horsepower, and engine speed. The fan spins rapidly, directing strong airflow at the car. The voice then states, "Next we'll see if the car reaches its speed of 300 kilometers per hour by accelerating quickly to 300 from stationary." The dashboard eventually shows 301 km/h and 1710 RPM, along with boost pressure, as the car accelerates, with the audio noting "From 0 to 185". The scene emphasizes precision engineering and high-tech performance testing.

A male narrator's voice, speaking in German and translated, confirms the test's success, stating, 'The tester is satisfied. Time was good, so it completed the test without incident, changes gears without any problem, through all the gears up to seventh gear after 300 km/h, it's all good.' The camera cuts to App Developer Dirk Berkenkopp, focused in the driver’s seat, as the narrator adds, 'It passed the test.' The scene transitions to an aerial shot of the large, modern Brabus Facility, a white-roofed building surrounded by greenery, then moves inside to the Brabus Classic Werk 4, where a more sophisticated musical theme begins. Here, the narrator explains, 'In contrast to Factory 3, there is a much slower pace in Factory 4. The Bottrop-based tuners also look after historic Mercedes-Benz vehicles.' The spacious, well-lit showroom is filled with meticulously maintained vintage Mercedes-Benz models, including a silver 300SL and a black 190E, as the narrator identifies it as 'The Brabus Classic Department,' which is 'both a showroom and a workshop.' The restoration...'

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a well-lit workshop, accompanied by upbeat, sophisticated instrumental music, where a Technician in Black Shirt, wearing a black Brabus-branded polo shirt, is inspecting the undercarriage of a brown vintage Mercedes-Benz elevated on a Hydraulic Lift. He points upward, possibly discussing the vehicle’s condition with a man in a blue suit who gestures thoughtfully. The scene shifts to a workbench where the same technician handles a large, complex suspension component. A male voiceover explains that Brabus restorations include effective anti-corrosion protection and a two-year warranty with unlimited kilometers. The camera then pans to a pristine showroom labeled 'BRABUS Classic Werk 4', which the voiceover states houses classic cars with a total value of around 20 million euros, including several examples of the 300 SL series. A lineup of meticulously restored vintage Mercedes-Benz models, including a vibrant orange, silver, and teal 300SL, are displayed under bright lighting, with close-ups highlighting chrome details, the iconic Mercedes-Benz grille, and the 'BRABUS Classic' badge. The voiceover specifically identifies the distinctive Gullwing from the 1950s as one of the most iconic vehicles, noting that restoration on one involves several thousand working hours and that these rare classics are sold for millions of euros. The camera focuses on a white Mercedes-Benz 300SL, with a Price Tag of 1,890,000.00 EUR visible on the windshield. The interior of a white classic car is shown, featuring a vintage dashboard with a 'Restaurationsfahrzeug' sign, which the voiceover identifies as a 50-year-old Mercedes-Benz 280 SL Pagoda awaiting its revival, with restored Pagoda cars achieving market prices in excess of 100,000 euros. The man in the blue suit walks through the showroom, inspecting the vehicles closely and leaning into the white car to examine its interior. The voiceover then begins to mention "The technical manager pays a visit before every...", implying the technician's significant role. The environment is clean, spacious, and professional, emphasizing the luxury and precision of Brabus Classic’s restoration work.

segments.integrated_caption: Ulrich Joachim Gauffrés, CEO of the Technics Department, dressed in a blue suit and glasses, meticulously inspects a white vintage Mercedes-Benz 300SL with its hood open in a bright, clean workshop. As he gestures and points to various engine components, a narrator explains that he's looking for specific pointers because not every vehicle is suitable for the ambitious revitalization process. Gauffrés then speaks in German, translated by the narrator, acknowledging that 'at first sight, this car looks terrible,' but quickly asserts it has a 'sound basis for restoration' because it hasn't been in an accident and possesses 'matching numbers.' He points to a stamped number '6082' on the inner edge of the hood, emphasizing the significance of original components like the engine, gearbox, and bonnet. He further highlights the last digit of the chassis number etched into the bonnet, stressing the importance of these matching numbers for a car that is not only brilliantly restored but also retains its original integrity. The camera shows close-ups of the aged but well-preserved engine bay and the classic interior with analog gauges and wooden trim, where a 'Restaurationsfahrzeug' sign rests on the windshield. The professional workshop, with large windows and a Brabus banner, underscores the high-end nature of the work. The narrator concludes by defining this meticulous process of reinstating old-timers to their original, as-new condition as 'six-star restoration' by the Bottrop team.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in the spacious, well-lit Brabus Classic Werk 4 showroom, displaying various vintage Mercedes-Benz models, including a silver 300SL and a black 190E. The focus shifts to a white vintage Mercedes-Benz 300SL with its hood open, revealing a detailed engine bay. Ulrich Joachim Gauffrés, CEO of the Technics Department, in a blue suit, inspects the car, while the audio states that "the vehicle is admittedly past its best, but it has the original features it had when it came off the production line." Gauffrés points to a stamped number '6082' on the inner hood and a 'Restaurationsfahrzeug' sign on the dashboard, as his voice highlights "the original welding points" as "a very good indicator that this car has definitely not been involved in an accident," calling it "a very important criterion." He then uses a handheld paint thickness gauge, displaying readings like '273' and '378', to inspect the car's body. The scene transitions to a well-lit, organized workshop where two technicians in Brabus-branded shirts work on a black vintage Mercedes-Benz; one is under the car on the suspension, while the other is at the engine bay. The narration explains that "the search for suitable base vehicles is a global one" and that "the luxury restoration then takes between 12 and 18 months," meticulously returning "chassis, engine, gearbox, and interior" – "every part, right up to the smallest piece of cable" – to its "original condition." The audio concludes by noting that "in the end, the 280SL will have a price tag of nearly 300,000 Euros," emphasizing that "the assembly of classic cars, faithful to the original, is a supreme discipline" demanding "very particular skills from the select employees," as "today, young mechatronics engineers often lack the necessary skill for this special art."

segments.integrated_caption: In a bright, clean Brabus workshop, Karl-Heinz Koch, Director of the Classic Workshop, is seen in a branded polo shirt, meticulously adjusting components on a disassembled engine block. The camera zooms in on the precision-machined cylinder bores and valve covers as Koch speaks, emphasizing that they work on "handmade" vehicles, where no two are alike, requiring a unique level of know-how that involves traditional skills like boring, sawing, and filing. Workshop sounds, including light clinking and tool use, underscore the activity. Other technicians in similar black Brabus shirts are busy with various engine parts, including a young trainee, Marc Schlechter, wearing blue nitrile gloves and a Brabus apron, assisting an older technician who uses a torque wrench. A narrator highlights that apprentices learn from 'old school chief vehicle mechanics' and that 'master and pupil benefit from each other.' Koch further elaborates on this dynamic, mentioning a technician (likely Marc Schlechter, the young trainee) who comes from South Africa and is learning German from him, while in return, he learns the tricks the younger technician has picked up over the years, describing it as a complementary relationship. The scene shifts to a brown vintage Mercedes-Benz elevated on a white hydraulic lift, where a man in a blue suit inspects it, as the narrator mentions 'the final inspection,' concluding a segment focused on professional, high-end classic car engineering and restoration.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a brown vintage Mercedes-Benz, described by a narrator as a "fully restored classic car" undergoing a "meticulous process" where "every detail needs to be perfect," even "under the car as well." The car is elevated on a white hydraulic lift, its undercarriage exposed, revealing gold-colored suspension parts, a central differential, and dual exhaust systems. Ulrich Joachim Gauffrés, CEO of the Technics Department, inspects the underside with a flashlight, while the narrator emphasizes the precision required for "the color of the screws on the originally restored horn, the surface of the underseal, the color of the axle parts," and that "every cable must be laid correctly and be made with the original material." Gauffrés then speaks to the camera in German, translated by the narrator as stating that "when a car leaves here, we're entitled to say we have done everything, truly everything, right down to the final screw to present a perfect car." Throughout this, a background music track plays. The scene transitions to an aerial view of the modern Brabus facility, referred to as "Factory 1," before moving inside to a clean, well-lit workshop. Here, a technician in a black Brabus shirt works on a gray Brabus G-Wagen in a showroom setting. The narrator explains that the "employee strips out everything from the vehicle's interior one piece at a time," as the technician precisely removes the driver’s seat, exposing the car’s floor and under-dash components, and then disassembles the center console, revealing wiring and internal mechanisms. The narrator further details that he "removes the side panel from the driver's door" and "dismantles all the seats," with other high-end vehicles like a beige G-Wagen and a black G-Wagen with a '263' tag visible in the background.

segments.integrated_caption: Against a backdrop of instrumental, cinematic music, the video opens with a technician in a black Brabus-branded shirt pushing a cart loaded with black car seats, bringing the vehicle parts to the upholstery shop. The scene transitions to a partially disassembled Brabus G-Wagen, its interior stripped to the metal frame, which the narrator explains is the 'bare car' that must wait for 4 to 5 weeks for its complete installation. A sign reading 'SATTLEREI' confirms the focus on interior craftsmanship, where more than 20 specialists work in the company's upholstery shop. Inside, technicians in black Brabus shirts meticulously stitch and assemble leather components at white tables with industrial sewing machines, custom-making new seat covers and trims for dashboards, doors, and headlining by hand. Close-ups show hands marking and cutting various leather pieces, which the narrator specifies are either elk, ostrich, mastic, or nubuck leather, available in around 3,500 different shades for the luxury finish. The process highlights the extensive upgrade, which includes not only visible parts but also the interiors of the glove compartment, various shelves, and a completely new floor. The video concludes by showcasing the finished, high-end Brabus interiors: a luxurious tan leather cabin with reclining seats and a built-in mini-bar, a modern white-and-black cockpit, and a sophisticated gray-and-black interior with Brabus-branded headrests, underscoring the transformation from raw materials to bespoke automotive luxury.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a luxurious car interior, showcasing a futuristic design with a starry ceiling, ambient lighting, and a sleek dashboard featuring a Mercedes-Benz logo, highlighting premium materials and advanced technology, including a large central display and a tablet. A narrator details how these interiors are "all embellished with decorative seams and artistically embroidered patterns." The scene transitions to a clean, well-lit workshop where Thomas Rockmann, a car interior decorator in a red polo shirt, meticulously works on a black leather car seat. The narrator explains that "employees take apart the seats" and that "modern car seats provide even greater comfort and therefore, have complex inner workings," including "electric seat adjustment, aircon, automatic headrests, and massage functions." Thomas carefully removes the outer shell, revealing the seat's intricate internal structure of metal frames, wiring, motors, connectors, and suspension systems. Speaking in German, Thomas explains the technical challenge of ensuring "all the functions working again as soon as we mount the seat cover back," specifically mentioning the need to reconnect the "seat heating mat" so "all the functions are displayed without anything missing or damaged." Close-up shots emphasize these internal mechanisms as Thomas manipulates components. The narrator adds that "the interior fitter needs around 30 minutes to take apart the seat." The segment concludes with Thomas reassembling the seat, demonstrating the careful attention to detail required in high-end automotive interior customization, with the workshop environment emphasizing precision and craftsmanship.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a white foam mold, intricately marked with red stitching patterns, serving as a template for car seat upholstery. As background music plays, a narrator explains that "his colleague," Ulla Owczarek, a seamstress, has already prepared the back seat, facing a "complicated task." Ulla, wearing a black Brabus-branded shirt with 'TEAM' and sponsor logos, meticulously places a large black leather piece over the foam, aligning it with precision. She uses a white chalk pencil to mark the leather along the foam's contours, ensuring accurate alignment. Ulla then explains in German, translated, that she's using "the old cover" to make a template because the customer wants it "just as it is stitched together here," but with "an additional tuck." The narrator clarifies that a "tuck is a purely decorative seam, small but beautiful." Ulla's detailed hand movements are captured as she traces curves and edges, then uses a yellow utility knife to trim excess leather along the marked lines. She further elaborates on the "tricky part" on the curve, emphasizing that if the curved line isn't followed correctly, "you lose the piping," and the part would be ruined and "you have to throw the part away." The scene concludes with a close-up of Ulla threading a sewing machine with white thread, preparing for assembly, as the narrator states she "separates the entire back seat cover" and "trims the leather for the new seam," noting that "a special sewing foot and a special needle are required." The clean, well-lit workshop environment, with industrial sewing machines and other technicians, underscores the professional and precise nature of the custom car interior fabrication.

segments.integrated_caption: The video segment opens with a Man in Red Polo Shirt walking through a clean, well-lit fabric storage area, meticulously browsing a wide array of hanging leather and textile samples in various colors including brown, yellow, blue, white, and black. As he carefully examines each material, running his hands over the surfaces to assess texture and quality, an informative voiceover emphasizes the critical importance of high-quality leather for tuners, detailing that the surface should be natural, soft, and breathable with a naturally occurring grain pattern, distinct from the engraved patterns of mass production. The camera closely follows his movements, focusing on his hands as he pulls out and inspects a dark gray textured leather, then a black pebbled finish, holding them up and turning them to inspect from multiple angles. The voiceover further explains that unlike standard leather with its evenly applied, coated grain, their leather reflects the natural aspect of the skin, showing variations from the center to the edge. His focused and analytical expression, set against a backdrop of industrial shelving and a color swatch board, clearly indicates a material selection process for car interiors. The audio elaborates on the natural imperfections like nicks, injuries, insect bites, and scars that necessitate cutting more from the center of the skin, resulting in a high waste factor, underscoring the meticulousness of the selection process for a Brabus vehicle.

He meticulously trims the excess leather with orange-handled scissors, accompanied by a cutting sound, completing the upholstery of the component. The professional workshop environment, with white workbenches and other technicians, underscores the precision involved in crafting custom leather components for high-end vehicles.

As the camera zooms in, these strands suddenly illuminate with vibrant red lights, creating a dynamic, starry effect, which the narrator explains is a 'red starry sky' for passengers, costing 'just under 20,000 euros.' A technician in a black Brabus-branded shirt is seen reaching up to adjust or inspect the fiber-optic system, demonstrating the intricate installation. Another man in a red polo shirt observes the work from outside the vehicle. A second male voice is heard commenting on the process, noting that someone 'will be sitting there for a few hours, working on cutting everything to length.' The scene transitions to a montage of finished Brabus interiors, showcasing luxurious, high-end cabins with tan leather, wood trim, and ambient lighting, illustrating the transformation. The narrator explains that 'the team needs five weeks for a complete interior overhaul,' and such 'bespoke orders are routine for the tuning pros.' The final shot is a close-up of a tan leather seat with a quilted pattern and a small 'BRABUS' logo, emphasizing the craftsmanship, as the narrator concludes that 'very little can still surprise' them. Throughout the segment, an upbeat, slightly electronic background music track plays, accompanying the narration and visual details.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up beneath a black Brabus G-Wagen, elevated on a hydraulic lift in a bright, clean workshop, where Quality Inspector Klaus Pachall, identified by on-screen text and wearing a Brabus-branded coat, meticulously inspects the undercarriage with a flashlight. Upbeat, driving background music accompanies Klaus's voice as he explains he's "just checking here to see if the cabling and in particular this brake caliper that we have painted are tight" and "that we're not losing any hydraulic fluid here," stressing the critical importance of functional brakes for the test drive of "a vehicle of this kind with so much power." He points to the suspension, exhaust, drivetrain, wheel hub, and brake assembly, demonstrating precision. The scene transitions to Klaus at the side of the G-Wagen, using a tablet to reference specifications while inspecting the wheel and tire. A narrator's voice interjects, explaining that for "large-scale conversions," a quality inspector like Klaus performs "no more than one final inspection in a day" due to its "extensive and detailed" nature. The narrator emphasizes Klaus's "trained eye" focusing on the "hull," ensuring "the paint has to be unblemished and the clearances uniform," and paying "particular attention to the forged rims," especially given "a five-figure sum for a set of new tires." Klaus then operates the control panel to lower the vehicle before meticulously cleaning its exterior, including the rear exhaust tips and glossy alloy wheels, with a blue microfiber cloth, concluding the segment with final touches in the professional workshop environment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a clean, industrial wash bay, referred to by the narrator as a "workshop," where Kevin Kalinowski, an "experienced employee" wearing a black Brabus-branded shirt, gestures towards a metal rack holding large white SOHAX containers. A male voice with a German accent details their contents as "calcium remover, windscreen cleaner, liquid polish, rim cleaner, insect remover, and car shampoo," stored in colored solutions with orange spigots. Upbeat, slightly dramatic instrumental music plays as the scene transitions to Kevin meticulously hand-washing a glossy black Brabus 800 G-Wagen, which the narrator identifies as a "luxury car." He uses a high-pressure washer, creating a misty spray as he rinses the vehicle's rear and side, followed by a large green sponge to scrub its soapy surface, focusing on the grille and hood. The narrator emphasizes his care, stating "they can't afford any scratches to the paintwork this close to delivery," and that he cleans "1 millimeter at a time" using a "sponge, high-pressure washer, and polishing cloth," taking "1 hour for the extensive cleaning." Kevin, in a German-accented voice, shares, "I wash around 10 to 15 cars a day. It just depends on how busy it is." After scrubbing, he buffs the car's paint with a rotary polisher and white foam pad, making the surface increasingly reflective. The final shot shows the gleaming Brabus 800 G-Wagen, with engine sounds audible, being driven out of the wash bay and carefully positioned onto the ramp of a white transport trailer, part of a larger transport truck. The narrator explains this is to ensure the car "reaches its final destination undamaged" as it's "not driven to its owner," before the trailer door closes.

Water sprays forcefully, creating a misty effect as he cleans the vehicle’s rear and side. He then switches to a large green sponge, scrubbing the soapy surface of the G-Wagen’s rear and front, focusing on the grille and hood. The final shot shows the gleaming Brabus G-Wagen being driven out of the wash bay and onto a white transport trailer, with the driver visible inside. entity_library.Digital Oven Display: A black digital interface on the industrial oven showing temperature, timer, and fan settings, with clear icons and numerical values for precise control. The display reads '72.4°C' and '280 min' during the curing process.
entity_library.Gloved Hand: A hand wearing a white latex glove, pouring a dark liquid into the white bucket and later handling the cured component. The glove ensures safety and precision during chemical handling.
entity_library.Gray Bottle: A translucent gray plastic bottle containing a dark, viscous liquid, identified by the narrator as transparent hardener and later referred to as black resin, used to pour the substance into the white bucket. entity_library.Yellowish Cured Component: A yellowish, rigid component with a white top, removed from a black tray after curing, inspected by the technician, and later placed on a wooden board for further processing. entity_library.Black Tray: A black rectangular tray used to hold the yellowish cured component, placed on a wooden board and handled by the technician during the curing and inspection process.
entity_library.Engine Block: A large, precision-machined aluminum engine block with multiple cylinder bores, mounted on a stand, being worked on by technicians. Karl-Heinz Koch's dialogue highlights that work on such components is 'handmade' and requires specialized know-how, including skills like boring, sawing, and filing.
entity_library.Kai Baumann: A man with gray hair and glasses, wearing a black Brabus polo shirt with orange 'BRABUS' logos, speaking to the camera and supervising the CNC machining process, while a narrator describes the intricate engineering work.
entity_library.Blue-Handled Cart: A mobile cart with blue metal handles, used to transport the machined engine block after processing, featuring a flat top surface and storage compartments.
entity_library.Thomas Schreiber: A motor mechanic with short hair and glasses, wearing a black Brabus-branded polo shirt with orange 'BRABUS' logos on the sleeve, and blue nitrile gloves. He is actively operating the Sunnen CK-21 Brabus cylinder honing machine, securing the engine block with bolts and adjusting the honing tool. He speaks in a clear, informative voice, explaining the specialized equipment and the precision required for the honing process.
entity_library.Sunnen CK-21 Brabus Cylinder Honing Mach: A white industrial machine with a digital control panel, labeled 'SUNNEN' and 'CK-21 BRABUS', featuring a vertical honing tool, coolant system, and a flexible black hose for debris extraction, actively used to hone the cylinder bores of the engine block, its operation accompanied by distinct whirring and spraying sounds, as a narrator explains its function in creating the necessary cross grinding for oil film adhesion and low-friction piston movement.
entity_library.Blue Nitrile Gloves: A pair of blue disposable gloves worn by Thomas Schreiber, providing protection and precision during the handling of the engine block and honing tool.
entity_library.Hex Bolts: Silver hexagonal bolts used to secure the engine block to the honing machine. The audio explains these are part of a 'torque plate' setup, which simulates the tension of a fully assembled engine to prevent warping.
entity_library.Honing Tool: A specialized cylindrical tool attached to the Sunnen machine, described in the audio as having 'diamond bars.' It rotates and simultaneously moves in and out of the cylinder bore, smoothing the interior and removing 'hundredths of a millimeter' to achieve a perfect, cylindrical surface.
entity_library.Coolant System: A system integrated into the Sunnen machine that sprays a liquid coolant onto the cylinder bore during honing to reduce friction and heat, visible as a dark liquid flowing over the block.
entity_library.Sven Bruckschlegel: Head of Engine Design, wearing a black Brabus polo shirt, inspecting the engine block using a borescope connected to a Sony monitor, adjusting the tool and pointing to the screen to analyze the cylinder’s internal surface; his voice is heard speaking German, explaining that he is checking if the silicon crystals in the aluminum cylinder are sufficiently exposed.
entity_library.Borescope: A flexible inspection tool with a camera at the end, connected to the Sony monitor, used to visually inspect the internal surface of the engine’s cylinder bores, specifically to analyze the interaction between aluminum and silicon crystals and ensure proper silicon relief, as explained by the male voice.
entity_library.Sony Monitor: A gray monitor with 'BRABUS' branding on the top bezel and 'SONY' on the bottom, displaying a magnified, blue-tinted view of a cylinder bore's textured interior during borescope inspection.

entity_library.Digital Scale: A small, silver digital scale with a black display, used to weigh engine components like pistons, highlighted in the audio as part of the precise, low-tolerance manufacturing process.

entity_library.Dashboard: The car's digital dashboard displaying performance metrics such as speed (up to 301 km/h), RPM (up to 1710), and boost pressure, with a yellow and black color scheme.
